regions: New England and Mid-Atlantic: CT, MA, NY, PA, NJ
Upper Midwest: WI, MN, MI, IL, OH
Southern pine belt: GA, AL, MS, LA, NC, SC

what_they_did.daily_work: Framed houses, barns, churches, and commercial buildings using hand-cut timber and, later, dimensional lumber. Work ranged from rough framing — raising walls, setting joists, cutting rafters — to finish carpentry: hanging doors, fitting window sash, installing molding and stair rails. In rural areas, carpenters were generalists who also built coffins, repaired wagons, and joined barn-raising crews. Coastal and river-town carpenters often specialized in ship carpentry or boatbuilding. A journeyman carpenter was expected to read a plan, calculate board feet, and lay out a building from foundation to ridge.
what_they_did.seasonal_cycle: Heavy demand from April through November when weather permitted outdoor framing. Winter months brought layoffs or reduced indoor finish work. In southern states the season extended longer; in northern states, carpenters often took supplementary work (furniture repair, coffin-making) through the winter.
what_they_did.typical_hours: 10-hour days were standard before 1886. The 8-hour movement gained traction among carpenters early — the United Brotherhood of Carpenters launched its 8-hour campaign in 1890 — though many non-union carpenters continued working 10-hour days into the 1920s.

economics.typical_arrangement: Journeyman carpenters were paid a daily wage, typically by a master builder or contractor. Some carpenters worked independently on small jobs (a porch, a barn addition) and negotiated a contract price. Apprentices received room, board, and tools but no wages during their 3-4 year term.
economics.annual_income_range: 1860s: $1.50-$2.50/day. 1880s-1900s: $2.00-$3.50/day ($500-$900/year given seasonal unemployment). 1920s union scale: $8-$12/day in cities. 1940s-1950s: $1.50-$2.50/hour union rate. Annual earnings were always reduced by winter layoffs — a carpenter who earned $3/day in 1900 might only work 200 days, yielding $600/year.
economics.comparison: Carpenters were paid at or above the median for skilled trades. They earned roughly double the wage of a common laborer but less than bricklayers or stone masons. The gap widened after unionization — Brotherhood carpenters in 1910 averaged 20-30% more than non-union carpenters in the same city.
economics.debt_cycle: Not typically subject to debt peonage, but seasonal unemployment created chronic cash-flow problems. Many carpenters supplemented income by farming small plots, and tool debt was real — a full set of hand tools represented several weeks' wages.

hazards: Falls from scaffolding and roof framing (the leading cause of death and injury)
Struck by falling timber or materials
Chronic joint damage from repetitive hand-tool use
working_conditions.living_conditions: Urban carpenters lived in working-class neighborhoods, often renting. Rural carpenters frequently built their own homes — a carpenter's house was his advertisement. Itinerant carpenters followed building booms (railroad towns, mining camps, post-fire reconstruction) and lived in boarding houses or temporary camps.
working_conditions.family_involvement: Sons commonly apprenticed to carpenter fathers, making it one of the most hereditary trades. Wives and daughters did not typically work in carpentry, but a carpenter's wife often managed the household accounts for contract jobs.
working_conditions.autonomy: High autonomy compared to factory workers. A skilled carpenter directed his own work on the job site and could refuse unsafe conditions. Independent carpenters set their own schedules and negotiated their own prices. This autonomy diminished as large general contractors consolidated the building trades after 1900.

push_factors: Age and injury — carpentry was hard on the body past age 50
Competition from factory-produced building components (pre-cut framing, manufactured doors and windows)
Economic downturns halted construction (especially the 1890s depression and the 1930s)
why_they_left.what_replaced_them: Power tools, pre-fabricated components, and specialized subcontracting transformed the trade. The all-purpose carpenter who could build an entire house gave way to specialists — framers, roofers, trim carpenters — each doing one phase. The craft survived but the generalist journeyman largely did not.
why_they_left.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} listed 'carpenter' on the census in {county} around {year}, it meant more than a job — it meant a set of skills passed hand to hand, a toolkit worth a month's wages, and the knowledge that every building in town bore the mark of someone who could read a plan and raise a wall.

records_generated.record_type: United Brotherhood of Carpenters and Joiners membership rolls
records_generated.what_it_shows: Name, local number, initiation date, dues payments, sometimes occupation specialty and employer
records_generated.where_to_find: UBC General Office Archives (Las Vegas, NV), state labor archives, university labor collections
records_generated.research_tip: Local union records are scattered — check state historical societies and university special collections in the state where your ancestor worked. Many locals published anniversary booklets listing charter members.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Name of builder or contractor, property owner, location, estimated cost, date of construction
records_generated.where_to_find: City or county clerk's office, municipal archives, county recorder of deeds
records_generated.research_tip: Building permits became common in cities after 1880 but were rare in rural areas. If your ancestor built in a city, the permit may name them as contractor. In rural areas, look for construction contracts filed with county courts.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Name of apprentice, name of master, term of service, skills to be taught, provisions for education
records_generated.where_to_find: County court records (orphans' court, probate court), state archives, historical societies
records_generated.research_tip: Formal indentures were most common before 1860. After the Civil War, apprenticeship became less formal, but some cities maintained apprenticeship boards whose records survive.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Name, occupation ('carpenter,' 'builder,' 'contractor'), home address, sometimes employer name
records_generated.where_to_find: State libraries, local public libraries, Ancestry.com and FamilySearch digitized collections
records_generated.research_tip: City directories are annual and can track a carpenter's career progression — from 'carpenter' to 'builder' to 'contractor' — and residential mobility year by year.

counter_narratives.claim: Carpentry was not an exclusively white occupation
counter_narratives.context: Black carpenters were numerous throughout the South, both during and after slavery. Enslaved carpenters built many of the plantation houses and public buildings of the antebellum South. After emancipation, Black carpenters continued to dominate the building trades in many southern cities until unions and licensing boards began excluding them in the early 1900s. In 1890, roughly 25% of carpenters in southern cities were Black.

counter_narratives.claim: Carpentry was not always a stable middle-class occupation
counter_narratives.context: The seasonal nature of construction meant chronic unemployment. A carpenter might earn a good daily wage but work only 180-220 days per year. Winters could be desperate, and economic downturns eliminated building work entirely. The 1893 depression and 1930s saw mass unemployment among carpenters, many of whom lost homes and tools.

counter_narratives.claim: Not all carpenters were independent craftsmen
counter_narratives.context: By the early 1900s, most urban carpenters worked for general contractors and had little more autonomy than factory hands. The romantic image of the self-employed master builder applied mainly to rural and small-town carpenters, and even they increasingly depended on lumber dealers' credit and factory-made components.

source_references.author: Christie, Robert A.
source_references.title: Empire in Wood: A History of the Carpenters' Union
source_references.notes: Definitive history of the United Brotherhood of Carpenters and Joiners, with extensive detail on wages, working conditions, and jurisdictional disputes

source_references.author: Haber, William
source_references.title: Industrial Relations in the Building Industry
source_references.notes: Comprehensive economic study of construction trades including detailed wage data and seasonal employment patterns for carpenters

source_references.author: Du Bois, W. E. B.
source_references.title: The Negro Artisan
source_references.notes: Atlanta University study documenting Black craftsmen including carpenters, with data on employment, wages, and union exclusion

source_references.author: Gaillard, Frye
source_references.title: If I Were a Carpenter: Twenty Years of Habitat for Humanity
source_references.notes: Useful for understanding the cultural weight of carpentry in American self-image, though focused on a later period
